title: Meet the Syntopicon: The Ambitious Index That Tried to Organize All of Western Thought (1952)
description:
in Books, History |   	July 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Mor┬Łtimer J. Adler rose to cul┬Łtur┬Łal promi┬Łnence in the mid-twen┬Łti┬Łeth-cen┬Łtu┬Łry Unit
body:
in Books, History |   	July 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Mor┬Łtimer J. Adler rose to cul┬Łtur┬Łal promi┬Łnence in the mid-twen┬Łti┬Łeth-cen┬Łtu┬Łry Unit┬Łed States, not that a fig┬Łure like him could have done so in any oth┬Łer place or time. A hap┬Łhaz┬Łard pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łal and intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal path involv┬Łing┬Ācopy-boy work at the New York┬ĀSun, night school, and an incom┬Łplete Colum┬Łbia degree even┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łly led to a fac┬Łul┬Łty posi┬Łtion teach┬Łing phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy of law at the Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty of Chica┬Łgo. In 1945, he com┬Łmenced work on what would become the Great Books of the West┬Łern World, a 54-vol┬Łume set pub┬Łlished by Ency┬Łclop├”┬Łdia Bri┬Łtan┬Łni┬Łca includ┬Łing the works of every┬Łone from Homer to Vir┬Łgil to Dar┬Łwin to Hem┬Łing┬Łway. Sold door-to-door, it became an unlike┬Łly suc┬Łcess by the ear┬Łly nine┬Łteen-six┬Łties, and for a time it was a fair┬Łly com┬Łmon, if book┬Łshelf-dom┬Łi┬Łnat┬Łing, sight in the aspi┬Łra┬Łtional homes of sub┬Łur┬Łban Amer┬Łi┬Łca.
How many of those fam┬Łi┬Łlies reg┬Łu┬Łlar┬Łly pulled their Great Books off the shelf is anoth┬Łer mat┬Łter. Despite hav┬Łing come through an inten┬Łsive process of cura┬Łtion, they could still look rather impos┬Łing as the wall of knowl┬Łedge they formed all togeth┬Łer. To this prob┬Łlem, Adler offered a char┬Łac┬Łter┬Łis┬Łti┬Łcal┬Łly ambi┬Łtious and idio┬Łsyn┬Łcrat┬Łic solu┬Łtion: a con┬Łcept-ori┬Łent┬Łed index called the Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon ŌĆö or rather, ŌĆ£A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£He believed these two vol┬Łumes to be just the ŌĆśassis┬ŁtanceŌĆÖ that the aver┬Łage man need┬Łed to dig into the books that formed West┬Łern Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion,ŌĆØ writes Jonathan White, an alum┬Łnus of the sim┬Łi┬Łlar┬Łly West┬Łern canon-based St. JohnŌĆÖs Col┬Łlege. They┬ĀŌĆ£com┬Łprised an exhaus┬Łtive cat┬Ła┬Łlogue of each time one of the 102 ŌĆśGreat Ideas of West┬Łern Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬ŁtionŌĆÖ was men┬Łtioned in the 431 ŌĆśGreat BooksŌĆÖ enshrined in Bri┬Łtan┬Łni┬ŁcaŌĆÖs col┬Łlec┬Łtion.ŌĆØ

Good and evil, log┬Łic and love, plea┬Łsure and pain, uni┬Łver┬Łsal and par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar: all the big ideas, at least as Adler defined them, were there in A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon. Cus┬Łtomers report┬Łed┬Łly found it unwieldy, but the notion behind it holds out a cer┬Łtain appeal still today. ItŌĆÖs even inspired the launch of Syntopi.com, a dig┬Łi┬Łtal suc┬Łces┬Łsor that enables you to nav┬Łi┬Łgate ŌĆ£the Great Con┬Łver┬Łsa┬ŁtionŌĆØ in a vari┬Łety of ways includ┬Łing a 3D visu┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion and a per┬Łson┬Łal cur┬Łricu┬Łlum-cre┬Łation tool. The Great Books of the West┬Łern WorldŌĆÖs mid-cen┬Łtu┬Łry read┬Łers ŌĆö pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łals and busi┬Łness┬Łmen look┬Łing to fill the gaps in their gen┬Łer┬Łal knowl┬Łedge, vet┬Łer┬Łans ready to learn more after their G.I. Bill-fund┬Łed col┬Łlege edu┬Łca┬Łtion, house┬Łwives hop┬Łing to get a han┬Łdle on what intel┬Łli┬Łgent peo┬Łple were sup┬Łposed to know about ŌĆö could have had fun with it. And we could ben┬Łe┬Łfit, no doubt, from redis┬Łcov┬Łer┬Łing a lit┬Łtle of their earnest┬Łly self-improv┬Łing spir┬Łit our┬Łselves. You can view an edi┬Łtion of A Syn┬Łtopi┬Łcon on the Inter┬Łnet Archive, or this site.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
The West┬Łern Canon: From Homer to Mil┬Łton (Free Course)
Harold Bloom Cre┬Łates a Mas┬Łsive List of Works in The ŌĆ£West┬Łern CanonŌĆØ: Read Many of the Books Free Online
The West┬Łern Tra┬Łdi┬Łtion by Eugen Weber: 52 Video Lec┬Łtures
Great Big Ideas: Free Course Fea┬Łtures Top Thinkers Tack┬Łling the WorldŌĆÖs Most Impor┬Łtant Ideas in 12 Lec┬Łtures
48 Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Videos Explain the His┬Łto┬Łry of Ideas: From Aris┬Łto┬Łtle to Sartre
The 10 Great┬Łest Books Ever, Accord┬Łing to 125 Top Authors (Down┬Łload Them for Free)
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Isaac Asimov Laments the ŌĆ£Cult of IgnoranceŌĆØ in the United States (1980)
description:
in Education, Politics, Sci Fi |   	July 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Rochester Insti┬Łtute of Tech┬Łnol┬Ło┬Łgy, via Wiki┬Łme┬Łdia Com┬Łmons
In 1980, s
body:
in Education, Politics, Sci Fi |   	July 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Rochester Insti┬Łtute of Tech┬Łnol┬Ło┬Łgy, via Wiki┬Łme┬Łdia Com┬Łmons
In 1980, sci┬Łen┬Łtist and writer Isaac Asi┬Łmov argued in an essay that ŌĆ£there is a cult of igno┬Łrance in the Unit┬Łed States, and there always has been.ŌĆØ That year, the Repub┬Łli┬Łcan Par┬Łty stood at the dawn of the Rea┬Łgan Rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion, which ini┬Łti┬Łat┬Łed a decades-long con┬Łser┬Łv┬Ła┬Łtive groundswell. Polit┬Łi┬Łcal strate┬Łgist Steve Schmidt (who has been regret┬Łful about choos┬Łing Sarah Palin as John McCainŌĆÖs run┬Łning mate in 2008) once point┬Łed to what he called ŌĆ£intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal rotŌĆØ as a pri┬Łma┬Łry cul┬Łprit, and a cult-like devo┬Łtion to irra┬Łtional┬Łi┬Łty among a cer┬Łtain seg┬Łment of the elec┬Łtorate.
ItŌĆÖs a famil┬Łiar con┬Łtention. There have been cri┬Łtiques of Amer┬Łi┬Łcan anti-intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łism since the countryŌĆÖs found┬Łing, though whether or not that phe┬Łnom┬Łe┬Łnon has inten┬Łsi┬Łfied, as┬ĀSusan Jaco┬Łby alleged in The Age of Amer┬Łi┬Łcan Unrea┬Łson, may be a sub┬Łject of debate. Not all of the unrea┬Łson is par┬Łti┬Łsan, as fail┬Łures to chal┬Łlenge human- and AI-gen┬Łer┬Łat┬Łed mis┬Łin┬Łfor┬Łma┬Łtion in polit┬Łi┬Łcal news sources and social media out┬Łlets over recent years have shown. But ŌĆ£the strain of anti-intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łism,ŌĆØ writes Asi┬Łmov, ŌĆ£has been a con┬Łstant thread wind┬Łing its way through our polit┬Łi┬Łcal and cul┬Łtur┬Łal life, nur┬Łtured by the false notion that democ┬Łra┬Łcy means that ŌĆśmy igno┬Łrance is just as good as your knowl┬Łedge.ŌĆÖŌĆØ
AsimovŌĆÖs pri┬Łma┬Łry exam┬Łples hap┬Łpen to come from the polit┬Łi┬Łcal world. How┬Łev┬Łer, he doesnŌĆÖt name con┬Łtem┬Łpo┬Łrary names but reach┬Łes back to take a swipe at Eisen┬Łhow┬Łer (ŌĆ£who invent┬Łed a ver┬Łsion of the Eng┬Łlish lan┬Łguage that was all his ownŌĆØ) and George Wal┬Łlace. Par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar┬Łly inter┬Łest┬Łing is AsimovŌĆÖs take on the ŌĆ£slo┬Łgan on the part of the obscu┬Łran┬Łtists: ŌĆśDonŌĆÖt trust the experts!ŌĆÖŌĆØ This lan┬Łguage, along with charges of ŌĆ£elit┬Łism,ŌĆØ Asi┬Łmov wry┬Łly notes, is so often used by peo┬Łple who are them┬Łselves experts and elites, ŌĆ£feel┬Łing guilty about hav┬Łing gone to school.ŌĆØ So many of the Amer┬Łi┬Łcan polit┬Łi┬Łcal classŌĆÖ wounds are self-inflict┬Łed, he sug┬Łgests, but thatŌĆÖs because they are behold┬Łen to a large┬Łly igno┬Łrant elec┬Łtorate:
To be sure, the aver┬Łage Amer┬Łi┬Łcan can sign his name more or less leg┬Łi┬Łbly, and can make out the sports headlinesŌĆöbut how many nonelit┬Łist Amer┬Łi┬Łcans can, with┬Łout undue dif┬Łfi┬Łcul┬Łty, read as many as a thou┬Łsand con┬Łsec┬Łu┬Łtive words of small print, some of which may be tri┬Łsyl┬Łlab┬Łic?
AsimovŌĆÖs exam┬Łples are less than con┬Łvinc┬Łing: road signs ŌĆ£steadi┬Łly being replaced by lit┬Łtle pic┬Łtures to make them inter┬Łna┬Łtion┬Łal┬Łly leg┬Łi┬ŁbleŌĆØ has more┬Āto do with lin┬Łguis┬Łtic diver┬Łsi┬Łty than illit┬Łer┬Ła┬Łcy, and accus┬Łing tele┬Łvi┬Łsion com┬Łmer┬Łcials of speak┬Łing their mes┬Łsages out loud instead of using print┬Łed text on the screen seems to fun┬Łda┬Łmen┬Łtal┬Łly mis┬Łun┬Łder┬Łstand the nature of the medi┬Łum. Jaco┬Łby in her book-length study of the prob┬Łlem looks at edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal pol┬Łi┬Łcy in the Unit┬Łed States, and the resis┬Łtance to nation┬Łal stan┬Łdards that vir┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łly┬Āensures wide┬Łspread pock┬Łets of igno┬Łrance all over┬Āthe coun┬Łtry. AsimovŌĆÖs very short, pithy essay has nei┬Łther the space nor the incli┬Łna┬Łtion to con┬Łduct such analy┬Łsis.
Instead he is con┬Łcerned with atti┬Łtudes. Not only are many Amer┬Łi┬Łcans bad┬Łly edu┬Łcat┬Łed, he writes, but the broad igno┬Łrance of the pop┬Łu┬Łla┬Łtion in mat┬Łters of ŌĆ£sci┬ŁenceŌĆ” math┬Łe┬Łmat┬ŁicsŌĆ” eco┬Łnom┬ŁicsŌĆ” for┬Łeign lan┬ŁguagesŌĆ”ŌĆØ has as much to do with Amer┬Łi┬ŁcansŌĆÖ unwill┬Łing┬Łness to read as their┬Āinabil┬Łi┬Łty.
There are 200 mil┬Łlion Amer┬Łi┬Łcans who have inhab┬Łit┬Łed school┬Łrooms at some time in their lives and who will admit that they know how to readŌĆ” but most decent peri┬Łod┬Łi┬Łcals believe they are doing amaz┬Łing┬Łly well if they have cir┬Łcu┬Łla┬Łtion of half a mil┬Łlion. It may be that only 1 per centŌĆöor lessŌĆöof Amer┬Łi┬Łcans make a stab at exer┬Łcis┬Łing their right to know. And if they try to do any┬Łthing on that basis they are quite like┬Łly to be accused of being elit┬Łists.
One might in some respects charge Asi┬Łmov him┬Łself of elit┬Łism when he con┬Łcludes, ŌĆ£We can all be mem┬Łbers of the intel┬Łlec┬Łtu┬Łal elite.ŌĆØ Such a blithe┬Łly opti┬Łmistic state┬Łment ignores the ways in which eco┬Łnom┬Łic elites active┬Łly manip┬Łu┬Łlate edu┬Łca┬Łtion pol┬Łi┬Łcy to suit their inter┬Łests, crip┬Łple edu┬Łca┬Łtion fund┬Łing, and oppose efforts at free or low cost high┬Łer edu┬Łca┬Łtion. Many efforts at spread┬Łing knowledgeŌĆölike the Chau┬Łtauquas of the ear┬Łly 20th cen┬Łtu┬Łry, the edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal radio pro┬Łgrams of the 40s and 50s, and the pub┬Łlic tele┬Łvi┬Łsion rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion of the 70s and 80sŌĆöhave been ad hoc and near┬Łly always imper┬Łiled by fund┬Łing crises and the designs of prof┬Łi┬Łteers.
Nonethe┬Łless, the wide┬Łspread (though hard┬Łly uni┬Łver┬Łsal) avail┬Łabil┬Łi┬Łty of free resources on the inter┬Łnet has made self-edu┬Łca┬Łtion a real┬Łi┬Łty for many peo┬Łple, and cer┬Łtain┬Łly for most Amer┬Łi┬Łcans. But per┬Łhaps not even Isaac Asi┬Łmov could have fore┬Łseen the bit┬Łter polar┬Łiza┬Łtion and dis┬Łin┬Łfor┬Łma┬Łtion cam┬Łpaigns that tech┬Łnol┬Ło┬Łgy has also enabled. Need┬Łless to say, ŌĆ£A Cult of Igno┬ŁranceŌĆØ was not one of AsimovŌĆÖs most pop┬Łu┬Łlar pieces of writ┬Łing. First pub┬Łlished on Jan┬Łu┬Łary 21, 1980 in Newsweek, the short essay has nev┬Łer been reprint┬Łed in any of AsimovŌĆÖs col┬Łlec┬Łtions.
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
Isaac Asi┬Łmov Reviews George OrwellŌĆÖs┬ĀNine┬Łteen Eighty-Four┬Āand Calls It ŌĆ£Not Sci┬Łence Fic┬Łtion, But a Dis┬Łtort┬Łed Nos┬Łtal┬Łgia for a Past that Nev┬Łer WasŌĆØ
Isaac Asi┬Łmov Pre┬Łdicts the Future on┬ĀThe David Let┬Łter┬Łman Show┬Ā(1980)
Isaac Asi┬Łmov on How Libraries Can Rad┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly Change Your Life (1971)
Isaac Asi┬Łmov Pre┬Łdicts in 1964 What the World Will Look Like in 2014
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.┬Ā

title: The Thai Beef Noodle Soup That Has Been Continuously Simmering for 52 Years
description:
in Food & Drink, Travel |   	July 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

As Bangkok ris┬Łes into the ranks of the worldŌĆÖs great culi┬Łnary des┬Łti┬Łna┬Łtio
body:
in Food & Drink, Travel |   	July 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

As Bangkok ris┬Łes into the ranks of the worldŌĆÖs great culi┬Łnary des┬Łti┬Łna┬Łtion cities, its restau┬Łrant scene caters to ever more well-heeled trav┬Łel┬Łers. There, you can now vis┬Łit estab┬Łlish┬Łments with not just one, and not just two, but three Miche┬Łlin stars. Even so, many a Bangkok habitu├® will sure┬Łly tell you that the cityŌĆÖs best food is still served in the same hum┬Łble places as always, or at least whose rent has┬ŁnŌĆÖt been hiked too bad┬Łly. Even in as hip┬Łster┬Łized an area as Ekka┬Łmai Road, though, some have been around long enough to own their real estate. Take Wat┬Łtana Panich, which has been serv┬Łing beef noo┬Łdle soup in its own build┬Łing for more than 50 years ŌĆö and indeed, using the same broth the whole time.
You can have a look at the process in the Great Big Sto┬Łry video at the top of the post. ŌĆ£For┬Łev┬Łer soup, also known as per┬Łpet┬Łu┬Łal stew or hunterŌĆÖs pot, is enjoy┬Łing a moment as adven┬Łtur┬Łous cooks and intre┬Łpid din┬Łers redis┬Łcov┬Łer the old method in which a broth can sim┬Łmer for weeks, months or even years,ŌĆØ writes Shan Li in a recent┬ĀWall Street Jour┬Łnal arti┬Łcle.
Third-gen┬Łer┬Ła┬Łtion Wat┬Łtana Panich own┬Łer Nat┬Łtapong Kawee┬Łnunta┬Łwong ŌĆ£has tend┬Łed the broth from morn┬Łing until night since gain┬Łing cus┬Łtody two decades ago. By day, it bub┬Łbles in a giant stain┬Łless-steel pot about 5 feet across and one foot deep, encased in lava-like con┬Łcrete and heat┬Łed by gas. He tweaks the fla┬Łvor by adding fresh ingre┬Łdi┬Łents, includ┬Łing fish sauce, soy sauce, chunks of beef and sachets of Chi┬Łnese herbs.ŌĆØ
Per┬Łhaps you feel you can taste it already. But its reg┬Łu┬Łlar vis┬Łi┬Łtors may insist that youŌĆÖll nev┬Łer real┬Łly know the fla┬Łvor of the shopŌĆÖs epony┬Łmous broth, con┬Łtin┬Łu┬Łous┬Łly refined while being rolled over night after night for five decades, until you try it for your┬Łself. The prospect may put cer┬Łtain West┬Łern┬Łers, uncom┬Łfort┬Łable con┬Łsum┬Łing even last nightŌĆÖs left┬Łovers, ill at ease. But they should rest assured that the sol┬Łid ingre┬Łdi┬Łents are always fresh. ItŌĆÖs just the broth itself, rig┬Łor┬Łous┬Łly strained each night and boiled each day, that has been kept in use, tying the estab┬Łlish┬Łment to its own past in the same man┬Łner as its inher┬Łit┬Łed own┬Łer┬Łship. As with any fam┬Łi┬Łly busi┬Łness, of course, each gen┬Łer┬Ła┬Łtion gets com┬Łplete┬Łly dis┬Łplaced soon┬Łer or lat┬Łer, just as every mol┬Łe┬Łcule of ŌĆ£for┬Łev┬Łer soupŌĆØ at one time will, in the┬Ło┬Łry, have been con┬Łsumed by some lat┬Łer time. Is the broth Wat┬Łtana Panich uses today real┬Łly iden┬Łti┬Łcal to the one it start┬Łed with in 1974? ThatŌĆÖs a philo┬Łsoph┬Łi┬Łcal ques┬Łtion best saved for after the meal.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
Chow┬Łda!: Three Cen┬Łturies of Recipes Reveal the Rise of New EnglandŌĆÖs Finest Culi┬Łnary Export
When Al Capone Opened a Soup Kitchen Dur┬Łing the Great Depres┬Łsion: Anoth┬Łer Side of the Leg┬Łendary MobsterŌĆÖs Oper┬Ła┬Łtion
Allen GinsbergŌĆÖs Per┬Łson┬Łal Recipe for Cold Sum┬Łmer Borscht
How to Make the Old┬Łest Recipe in the World: A Recipe for Net┬Łtle Pud┬Łding Dat┬Łing Back 6,000 BC
The Old┬Łest Restau┬Łrant in the World: How MadridŌĆÖs Sobri┬Łno de Bot├Łn Has Kept the Oven Hot Since 1725
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Katherine Wolkoff Meditates on Absence in Her Tender Photos of Deer Beds
description: The last few mornings, as IŌĆÖve walked with my dog up the ravine behind my house, two fawns seem to bound of thin air, racing in unison through the
body:
The last few mornings, as IŌĆÖve walked with my dog up the ravine behind my house, two fawns seem to bound of thin air, racing in unison through the trees until far enough way that they stop, stare, and wait for us to pass. ItŌĆÖs not uncommon to see several does grazing in the same woods, and IŌĆÖve always wondered where they sleep. Photographer Katherine Wolkoff followed a similar curiosity as she traversed the grassy meadows of Block Island, which sits a few miles off the coast of Rhode Island, for her series Deer Beds.
Flattened by lean cervid bodies, tall grasses reveal the areas where deer bed down. They donŌĆÖt typically sleep in the same place every single night, but a home range area may have several spots that they return to repeatedly. Wolkoff prints the images at nearly life size, focusing directly on the nest-like spaces in intimate, horizonless meditations on comfort, presence, care, and resilience.

When the series was first exhibited, critic Eva Diaz noted in Artforum that ŌĆ£The prevailing metaphor of photography is that of the hunt. Photographers shoot, even stalk, their subjects; in the case of Katherine WolkoffŌĆÖs work, the absence of ŌĆśpreyŌĆÖ itself becomes the subject of the project.ŌĆØ Sometimes during a walk, the artist encounters deer nestled in the grass and they dart away, startled. Other times, the beds are already empty.
ŌĆ£My mother, a science teacher, first mentioned deer beds to me, and I began walking the fields, following deer paths to find them,ŌĆØ Wolkoff tells Colossal. ŌĆ£That solitary, meditative search is still central to how I work today.ŌĆØ Broadly, her work focuses on the natural world in the Anthropocene, plumbing the relationship between humans and the land in light of the ongoing climate crisis.
The artist is currently finishing a book of pinhole photographs taken from the perspective of migrating birds on Block Island. ŌĆ£The resulting pictures have a blurred, frantic quality that I think of as visualizing the birdsŌĆÖ depletion: the chaos of an animal pushed to its limits over open water and unfamiliar coastline, flying through the night with no guarantee of where it will land or whether it will survive the crossing,ŌĆØ she says. Some of these works will be part of a forthcoming exhibition centered around the Atlantic Flyway at Benrubi Gallery next spring.

schema.headline: Katherine Wolkoff Meditates on Absence in Her Tender Photos of Deer Beds

title: Mark Rothko's Color Fields Mirror the Elements in a 'Weather-to-Painting Experiment'
description: Mark Rothko is known for his ŌĆ£color fieldŌĆØ paintings, a genre that was coined in the 1950s to describe his work specifically, along with peers lik
body:
Mark Rothko is known for his ŌĆ£color fieldŌĆØ paintings, a genre that was coined in the 1950s to describe his work specifically, along with peers like Barnett Newman and Clyfford Still. These works are generally characterized by their total abstraction and emphasis on clearly delineated areas, or ŌĆ£fields,ŌĆØ of different hues. One might also think of Josef AlbersŌĆÖ seminal series titled Homage to the Square, which delved into the virtually infinite relationships between colors.
For Rothko, canvases were often very large, measuring upwards of 10 feet. The works inside the Rothko Chapel in Houston, for example, are architectonic, commanding the viewerŌĆÖs complete attention and inviting us to slow down and truly immerse ourselves in a contemplative space. Even on social media, a new generation is discovering the artistŌĆÖs meditative works as an antidote to visual and information overload. His oeuvre also forms the basis of a project called Current Rothko by New York-based designer and creative director Joonas Virtanen.

On the elegantly simple website, type in any location in the world and the algorithm will select a Rothko painting representative of the weather there. Currently, the catalogue contains 89 paintings, each of which are tagged with a color register, brightness, temperature, and mood so that the search engine can match them to the conditions of specific places at any moment.
Virtanen has sourced many of the images from WikiArt and WikiMedia, plus fair-use image databases from major institutions like the National Gallery of Art, MoMA, Tate, and others. RothkoŌĆÖs work remains copyrighted, as artworks generally enter the public domain after 95 years. Access to high-quality imagery is therefore limited to what Virtanan describes as small ŌĆ£cappedŌĆØ previews that could never be reproduced at scale.
While each image is accompanied by the title of work, the designer acknowledges that the platform is a work in progress, and details such as dimensions and medium would enrich the experience. In the meantime, since I began this article, Chicago has transitioned from 77 degrees and clear with ŌĆ£No. 5/No. 22ŌĆØ (1950) to a cloudy, humid 81 degrees with a chance of ŌĆ£Black on Maroon (Seagram V)ŌĆØ (1958).
For another art historical twist on the weather, check out VirtanenŌĆÖs Plein Air weather visualizer. And if youŌĆÖre in New York City, you might also get a kick out of his subway status tool, Tangled Lines.

schema.headline: Mark Rothko's Color Fields Mirror the Elements in a 'Weather-to-Painting Experiment'

title: Scan More than 60 Million Stars in the Most Detailed Photo of the Milky Way Ever Taken
description: In March 2025, the Euclid mission led by the The European Space Agency (ESA) enabled scientists to capture the highest resolution image ever taken
body:
In March 2025, the Euclid mission led by the The European Space Agency (ESA) enabled scientists to capture the highest resolution image ever taken of the dense, glowing center of the Milky Way galaxy. An enormous swarm of stars forms a bulge at the heart of the spiral, and researchers continue to search amid these billions of gaseous orbs for exoplanets, or any planet thatŌĆÖs located outside of our solar system.
ŌĆ£The galactic bulgeŌĆöthe central region of our galaxyŌĆöis a vast, tightly packed structure filled mainly with old, cooler stars, giving it its characteristic yellow colour,ŌĆØ ESA says. The photograph, which is taken with visible light, allows scientists to pinpoint exoplanets and measure their mass by monitoring tiny changes in starlight over time in a process called microlensing.



ESAŌĆÖs Euclid image captures more than 60 million stars, plus other phenomena such as nebulae, bright star clusters, and molecular clouds that appear like amorphous, dark splotches in front of the brighter areas. Incidentally, to the human eye, they serve as a visual guide or cue to the incredible depth of field the image has actually captured.
ŌĆ£For comparison, EuclidŌĆÖs sharpness and sensitivity in visible light is similar to the┬ĀNASA/ESA Hubble Space TelescopeŌĆÖs┬Āwide field camera,ŌĆØ ESA says. ŌĆ£But each pointing that Euclid captures in a few hours spans an area 270 times larger than HubbleŌĆÖs field of view. To observe the same Euclid mosaic, the┬ĀKeck Observatory┬Āwould need around 2,000 hours.ŌĆØ
Zoom in on the Galactic Bulge Survey image on ESASky.
A molecular cloud in front of a field of stars
A nebula in the Milky WayŌĆÖs galactic bulge
A star cluster

schema.headline: Scan More than 60 Million Stars in the Most Detailed Photo of the Milky Way Ever Taken

title: Guimi You's Atmospheric Oil Paintings Delve into Memory, Introspection, and Rediscovery
description: Whether itŌĆÖs the atmosphere casting a haze or the fuzziness of memories and dreams, Guimi YouŌĆÖs lush paintings have an aura of wistfulness and qui
body:
Whether itŌĆÖs the atmosphere casting a haze or the fuzziness of memories and dreams, Guimi YouŌĆÖs lush paintings have an aura of wistfulness and quietude. The Seoul-based artist creates dreamy oil compositions that tap into personal experience, passing time, and how one gains perspective and reevaluates their needs or desires as they go through life.
YouŌĆÖs canvases are infused with elements of still life and landscape traditions, where anonymous protagonists reflect quietly in a garden, pause in a golden meadow, or stroll through a park in the rain. Cerulean shadows complement the magenta jacket of a woman strolling with her dog along a stream in ŌĆ£Spring Walk,ŌĆØ and a woman sits down at an easel in an open doorway in ŌĆ£Painting, Again.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£Park in the DrizzleŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 46 x 36 inches. Photo by Ian Yang
Several of the works seen here are included in YouŌĆÖs current solo exhibition, When the Sun Shines Again at Lehmann Maupin, a meditation on rediscovery and beginning again. ŌĆ£Conceived as a tribute to those who have returned to creating after time away, the works unfold through a recurring image of sunlight, an enduring symbol of clarity, renewal, and hope,ŌĆØ the gallery says.
The figures sometimes appear to blend into the backgrounds like secondary characters, while the spaces filled with objects, contrasting light, and portals take center stage. Individual faces are often obscured or shadowy, introspectively looking down or away. Some appear lost in thought, while others concentrate on a creative task, such as throwing pottery on a wheel or putting a brush to canvas. There is a somberness tinged with hope as You taps into the courage required to start anew.
The artistŌĆÖs approach, the gallery says, ŌĆ£synthesizes East Asian pictorial traditions of evocation and atmospheric transparency with Western lineages such as Romanticism and Surrealism, positioning landscape not as an escapist subject, but as a space for reflecting on memory, subjectivity, and how we situate ourselves within the world.ŌĆØ
When the Sun Shines Again continues through August 14 in New York City.
ŌĆ£Painting, AgainŌĆØ (2026). oil on linen, 65 x 81 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Spring WalkŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 65 x 81 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£PauseŌĆØ (2025), oil on linen, 65 x 80 inches. Photo by OnArt Studio
ŌĆ£The Shape of SilenceŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 81 7/8 x 65 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Let the Sunlight InŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 65 x 79 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£GoldenŌĆØ (2026), oil on linen, 74 x 59 7/8 x 1 1/8 inches. Photo by JeongKyun Goh
ŌĆ£Deep in the YellowŌĆØ (2025), oil on linen, 63 7/8 x 44 1/8 inches. Photo by OnArt Studio

schema.headline: Guimi You's Atmospheric Oil Paintings Delve into Memory, Introspection, and Rediscovery
